comments.title: State-transition factorization notation is ambiguous
comments.quote: State-transition function _T_[¯] = _Ts · TG_ where _Ts_ : _S × D × A_[¯] _→_ ∆( _S × D_ ) and _TG_ : _GL × A →_[¯] ∆( _GL_)
comments.explanation: The factorization T̄ = Ts · TG is presented as a simple product, but T̄ should be a distribution over S̄ = S × D × G_L given (s̄, ā). The product form implicitly assumes conditional independence between (s, d^s) transitions and G transitions given the action. This is plausibly true given that Ts depends only on (s, d^s, ā) and TG only on (G, ā), but the domains written for Ts and TG exclude G and (s, d^s) respectively, which effectively asserts this independence without stating it. A reader trying to reproduce the formalism might be confused about whether Ts can depend on the current goal stack (which would matter for whether the operation policy's action a^do depends on the top goal). Since a^do = arg max_a π̂(a | b^s_t, d^g_t) does depend on d^g_t from the stack, the domain of Ts as written seems correct for the state component, but the factorization deserves more explicit notation.

comments.title: Cost function domain inconsistent with cost expressions used
comments.quote: Cost function ¯ _c_ : ( _S×GL_ ) _×A →_[¯] R (defined in §4.4 to trade off acting cost and task error)
comments.explanation: The cost function is declared with domain (S × G_L) × Ā, but in §4.4 the task error for DONE is written as c(s_t, a_done), which uses only s_t (not the goal stack). That's fine since the goal stack is available in S × G_L. However, the subgoal task errors are explicitly excluded, which would require the cost function to know whether the current top goal is the main goal — this requires access to G_L, consistent with the declared domain. So on reflection this is consistent. However, note that the domain excludes D (the current description) while c (the underlying POMDP cost) is defined on S × A — this is consistent. No actual issue after checking.

comments.title: DONE subgoal condition contradicts earlier definition
comments.quote: If _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the agent may choose a new goal to follow.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as describing the case when the current goal is a subgoal, but the parenthetical says '_gt_ = _g_ 1' — the same condition used in the previous sentence to define the main goal case. For _gt_ to be a subgoal, the condition should be _gt_ ≠ _g_ 1. This appears to be a typo/sign error in the condition. It's local and easily fixable, but as stated it directly contradicts the preceding sentence and would confuse a reader trying to understand the semantics of DONE.

comments.title: Rule-based baseline description appears incomplete/ambiguous
comments.quote: We compare our RL-learned interaction policy with a rule-based baseline that first takes the GOAL action and then randomly selects actions.
comments.explanation: The baseline is said to 'randomly select actions' after an initial GOAL, yet it is then constrained by per-action budgets ⌊Xa⌋+y and a tie-breaking rule about DONE vs SUB. It is unclear from what distribution actions are sampled (uniform over remaining non-exhausted actions? weighted?), how ties are broken when multiple budgets are exhausted, and what happens when all budgets are exhausted before reaching a goal. For a baseline whose tuning on validation sets is used to match RL action counts, a reader would need these details to reproduce the result. This is an underspecification that affects interpretation of Table 1's comparison.

comments.title: Claim that human-assisted agent outperforms dense-information agent is not clearly supported by Table 1
comments.quote: This human-assisted agent even outperforms an agent that always has access to dense information in unseen environments, thanks to the ability to request subgoals.
comments.explanation: Checking Table 1 against this claim: 'Dense ds and dg' gives 9.4 (11.9) on UNSEENENV, while the RL-learned ψθ gives 19.8 (22.5). So the claim does hold numerically on UNSEENENV. However, on UNSEENSTR the dense-dense agent achieves 97.8 vs 85.8, and on UNSEENOBJ 81.7 vs 78.2 — so the human-assisted agent does NOT outperform the dense agent in general, only in unseen environments. The abstract/intro phrasing could be read as a general claim; the qualifier 'in unseen environments' does appear, so this is defensible, but readers skimming the abstract may miss that the result is specific to one of three test conditions.

comments.title: Inconsistency in DONE action's subgoal condition
comments.quote: If _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the agent may choose a new goal to follow.
comments.explanation: This appears to be an OCR or typo issue, but it's a real logical problem in the text: the condition for 'main goal' is _gt_ = _g_1, and the condition for 'subgoal' is also stated as _gt_ = _g_1. The subgoal case should presumably be _gt_ ≠ _g_1. Even accounting for OCR, both conditions are written identically in the passage, which would confuse a careful reader about when the episode ends vs when a new goal is chosen.

comments.title: Overclaim about 'doubling' dense-description policy performance
comments.quote: In unseen environments, with its capability of requesting subgoals, the agent impressively doubles the success rate of the operation policy that has access to dense descriptions.
comments.explanation: I tried to verify this claim against Table 2, but Table 2 reports stack size results (max 33.5% on UNSEENENV with stack 3, 21.6% with stack 2), not a direct comparison to a dense-description operation-policy baseline. The dense-_d^s_-and-_d^g_ baseline number is not shown in the passage provided, so I can't directly check it. However, the claim 'doubles' is strong and would benefit from a specific numerical reference. This is a potential overclaim if the dense baseline's UNSEENENV rate is not approximately half of the learned policy's rate. Since the paper elsewhere claims 7× improvement over unaided agent, and a dense agent would be much stronger than unaided, this 2× claim is plausible but cannot be verified from this passage alone.

comments.title: Interpretation of increasing SUB requests not supported by evidence
comments.quote: These results show that the critic model overestimates the _V_ values in states where SUB actions are taken, leading to the agent learning to request subgoals more than needed.
comments.explanation: This is a strong causal diagnostic claim (critic value overestimation) made without presenting any direct evidence about critic values. The observation is that with stack size 3, the agent makes 4.5 SUB requests per episode on UNSEENOBJ without improving performance. This is consistent with value overestimation but also consistent with other explanations (e.g., exploration noise, policy entropy, miscalibrated cost trade-off, or local optima in A2C training). Without presenting V-value statistics, loss curves, or an ablation, 'These results show that the critic model overestimates' is an overclaim — the results are consistent with that hypothesis but do not demonstrate it.

comments.title: Stack size 1 row shows more SUB actions than expected
comments.explanation: I checked this carefully: with stack size 1, SUB actions should be impossible (since SUB would require pushing a second element, violating |G|=L=1). The table correctly shows 0.0 SUB actions for stack size 1 — this is consistent. However, I noticed the GOAL count is 1.9 for stack size 1 but drops to 1.0 for sizes 2 and 3. The text earlier states 'The average number of GOAL actions does not vary, showing that the interaction policy has correctly learned that making more than one goal-clarifying request is unnecessary.' This is contradicted by the stack-size-1 row which shows 1.9 GOAL calls on average — almost twice. The claim that GOAL calls don't vary was made about the three evaluation conditions (UNSEENSTR/OBJ/ENV) at a fixed stack size, so it doesn't strictly contradict the table, but a careful reader comparing these numbers would notice the inconsistency in how the policy behaves across stack sizes without explanation.

comments.title: Success rate comparisons use different bases inconsistently
comments.quote: Aided by our RL-learned interaction policy, the agent observes a substantial _∼_ 2 _×_ increase in success rate on UNSEENSTR, _∼_ 5 _×_ on UNSEENOBJ, and _∼_ 7 _×_ on UNSEENENV, compared to when performing tasks using only the operation policy.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction state 'up to 7× improvement in task success rate' and this passage claims 2×/5×/7× improvements. I cannot verify the exact baselines from the passage, but these multiplicative claims depend strongly on the operation-policy-alone baseline numbers, which use sparse descriptions per the pre-training/training setup. The ratios are meaningful but the denominator (operation policy on sparse descriptions in unseen environments) may be very small, which makes large fold-increases easier to achieve while absolute success rates remain modest (e.g., 21.6% on UNSEENENV with stack 2). This isn't wrong but the 7× framing in the abstract slightly overstates what a reader should take away — it should ideally be paired with absolute numbers for context.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important and timely problem—agents that can recognize uncertainty and solicit targeted human assistance—and the proposed HARI framework is conceptually appealing, especially in distinguishing *when* to ask from *what* to ask and in allowing requests for current-state, goal-state, and subgoal information. The introduction is generally clear and motivates the work well, with a useful connection to cooperative communication and POMDP limitations, and the navigation example helps make the framework concrete. That said, the framing currently overstates the novelty and faithfulness of the communication mechanism relative to what is demonstrated: the “human” appears simulated, the request types are hand-designed and limited, and it is not yet clear that the agent is learning grounded communicative intent rather than optimizing over a small set of oracle-like information channels. The claims about rich human-agent communication and Gricean faithfulness therefore need to be tied more carefully to the actual experimental setup. A major concern is whether the framework’s empirical gains come from principled hierarchical interaction learning or from privileged access to structured side information, especially subgoals, whose generation process and realism must be scrutinized. The paper would benefit from sharper formal definitions early on, clearer separation between the general framework and the specific navigation instantiation, stronger discussion of assumptions about the assistant’s knowledge and reliability, and comparisons to simpler baselines such as fixed query schedules, uncertainty-triggered querying, or agents with equivalent additional observations. Overall, the paper has a promising idea and readable motivation, but its contribution will depend heavily on whether the formalization and experiments convincingly show that the learned interaction policy provides benefits beyond access to carefully engineered help signals.

comments.title: Goal-conditioned tasks are not fully encoded in the stated POMDP cost/state formalism
comments.quote: We consider an environment defined by a partially-observed Markov decision process (POMDP) E = (S, A, T, c, D, ρ) with state space S, action space A, state-transition function T : S × A → Δ(S), cost function c : S × A → R, description space D, and description function ρ : S → Δ(D).
comments.explanation: The setup appears to intend a goal-conditioned POMDP in which a sampled goal g1 and its description d[g]1 are passed separately to the policy. However, the formal POMDP defines a single task-independent cost c:S×A→R and later treats the belief as a distribution over S only. If tasks have different sampled goals, the objective “reach g1” cannot generally be represented by a single cost c(s,a) unless the goal is included in the state, the cost is goal-dependent, or an additional success/terminal condition is specified. A standard repair would be to augment the state with the task goal, define c(s,g,a), or explicitly state that the POMDP/cost is task-dependent.

comments.title: Trajectory indexing appears inconsistent with an H-step cost sum
comments.quote: The goal of the agent is to reach g1 with minimum cumulative cost C(τ) = ∑[H]t=1 c[t], where τ = (s1, d[s]1, a1, s2, d[s]2, a2, ..., sH, d[s]H) is an execution of the task.
comments.explanation: If an H-step rollout incurs costs for t=1,...,H and each action a_t causes a transition to s_{t+1}, the trajectory would normally include actions a_1,...,a_H and states through s_{H+1}, or else use an explicit terminal convention. As written, τ stops at (s_H,d[s]_H) and omits a_H and the post-action state/description, while C(τ) includes c_H. If the intended rollout has H observations and H−1 actions, the cost sum should be adjusted; otherwise the trajectory definition should include the final action and next state.

comments.title: The claimed limitation of the standard POMDP framework is overstated
comments.quote: Thus, the standard POMDP framework does not provide any mechanism for improving the agent’s performance other than enhancing the operation policy.
comments.explanation: The statement is reasonable for the restricted operation POMDP the authors define, which has a fixed description function, fixed initial goal description, and no query or assistant actions. But it is too broad as a claim about the standard POMDP framework itself. POMDPs can model information-gathering actions, active sensing, communication channels, or assistants by augmenting the state, action, and observation spaces. The paper should qualify the claim as applying to this fixed autonomous operation formulation rather than to POMDPs in general.

comments.title: The interaction POMDP transition is under-specified for both DO and SUB
comments.quote: (d) DO: executes the action a[do]t ≜ arg max_a∈A π̂(a | b[s]t, d[g]t). The agent transitions to a new operation state s[t+1] ∼ T(s[t], a[do]t); ... The SUB action pushes a new subtask (g[t+1], d[g][t+1]) to the stack. ... The goal-stack transition function TG is defined as TG(G[t+1] | G[t], ā[t]) = 1_{G[t+1] = G[t].update(ā[t])}.
comments.explanation: The high-level interaction environment is intended to be a POMDP, but the stated transition factorization omits variables needed to determine the next state. For DO, the actual low-level action is chosen by the operation policy as a function of its belief/memory and the current goal description, not merely by the abstract action DO and the true operation state. Thus the transition and cost for DO are not determined by the stated interaction state/action unless the operation belief or controller memory is included in the state, or DO is parameterized by an explicit low-level action. For SUB, the next subgoal is sampled from the assistant model, depending on the current operation state and goal, so the next stack is not a deterministic function of G_t and ā_t alone. A complete definition would need a transition such as T_G(G_{t+1} | s_t, G_t, ā_t) with the assistant-induced probabilities, and/or an augmented interaction state.

comments.title: Belief state is described as summarizing hidden states rather than observations
comments.quote: A belief state b̄t summarizes a partial execution (s̄1, ā1, · · ·, s̄t). We formally define the interaction policy as ψθ : B̄ → Δ(A), where B̄ is the set of all interaction belief states.
comments.explanation: In a POMDP, the agent's belief should summarize the observation/action history and represent a distribution over hidden states. The displayed partial execution contains interaction states s̄_t, which include hidden quantities such as the true operation state and true goal stack. Earlier observable executions are described in terms of descriptions and actions, not hidden states. This is likely a notation slip, but it makes the formal input to the interaction policy ambiguous. The definition should use observable descriptions, goal-description stacks, and actions as the history from which the belief is computed.

comments.title: Goal-description stack length is inconsistent with stacks containing at most L elements
comments.quote: Description space D̄ = D × G_L^d where G_L^d is the set of all goal-description stacks of size L. At any time, the agent cannot access the environment’s goal stack G, which contains true goal states. Instead, it can only observe the descriptions in G.
comments.explanation: The earlier state-space definition says that the true goal stack contains at most L elements, and the initial stack has only one element. Here the observable description stack is described as having size L. If stacks are fixed-length with padding, the padding value and its behavior under DONE/SUB should be specified. If stacks are variable-length up to depth L, then the description should say “of size at most L.” This matters because the observation space and boundary cases are part of the formal POMDP definition.

comments.title: Navigation action space is state-dependent and appears to omit termination
comments.quote: At any time, the agent’s operation action space A consists of traversing to any of the nodes that are adjacent to its current node.
comments.explanation: This is a common graph-navigation shorthand, but it conflicts with the earlier formalization of a fixed action space A and a policy π̂:B×D→Δ(A). If actions are adjacent nodes, the valid action set varies with the current true node, raising questions about how the policy outputs over variable action sets and whether the agent observes a navigability mask under partial observability. The sentence also appears to omit the special termination action introduced earlier. Even if HARI uses an interaction-level DONE action, the operation-policy pretraining and no-assistant baselines need a stopping mechanism or horizon convention.

comments.title: Goal-description feature representation is ambiguous for dense and sparse goals
comments.quote: A dense description of a current location contains the room name at the location, and the features of M objects restricted to be within δ meters of the location. The features of each object consists of (i) its name, (ii) horizontal and vertical angles (relative to the current viewpoint), and (iii) distance ... A dense description of a goal follows the same representation scheme. ... The sparse goal description (the task request) has only features of the target object and the room name where the object is located at.
comments.explanation: The coordinate frame for dense goal descriptions is unclear. For current-location descriptions, angles and distances are relative to the agent's current viewpoint; for a goal description, the corresponding viewpoint is not specified. If the frame is the goal viewpoint, that should be stated; if it is relative to the agent, it may reveal navigation-relevant geometry. The sparse description is also ambiguous because “features of the target object” could mean only the object category, or could include the same angle/distance features defined immediately above. This distinction matters because the sparse/dense information gap is central to the experiments.

comments.title: Adjacent-subgoal oracle actions conflict with the stated dense-description protocol
comments.quote: Especially, if a subgoal location is adjacent or coincides with the agent’s current location, instead of describing room and object features, the human specifies the ground-truth action to go to the subgoal ... The human always returns dense descriptions.
comments.explanation: For adjacent or coincident subgoals, the assistant returns a ground-truth low-level action rather than the dense room/object description otherwise described. This is a qualitatively different form of help and may be stronger than a state or goal description, especially for measuring the value of SUB requests. It is also unclear how action-valued goal descriptions are encoded for the pretrained operation policy, and what action is returned when the subgoal coincides with the current location. The later statement that the human always returns dense descriptions should acknowledge this exception or define these oracle actions as part of the dense-description format.

comments.title: UNSEENENV and “completely new” goal-description claims need clearer split definitions
comments.quote: The interaction policy is trained in environments that are previously seen as well as unseen during pre-training. Finally, in the evaluation phase, the interaction policy is tested on ... new environment (UNSEENENV). ... In contrast, the initial goal descriptions in UNSEENOBJ and UNSEENENV are completely new to the agent, thus gathering more information about them is necessary.
comments.explanation: It is unclear whether UNSEENENV is new relative only to operation-policy pretraining or also held out from interaction-policy training. Since the interaction policy is trained in environments that were unseen during pretraining, the reference point for “new environment” matters. The related claim that UNSEENENV initial goal descriptions are “completely new” is also stronger than the split alone implies: a new physical environment does not necessarily mean new sparse object or room categories. The paper should state precisely which components have seen which environments, object types, and goal descriptions.

comments.title: Rule-based baseline is underspecified despite being a key comparison
comments.quote: We compare our RL-learned interaction policy with a rule-based baseline that first takes the GOAL action and then randomly selects actions. In each episode, we enforce that the rule-based policy can take at most ⌊Xa⌋ + y actions of type a, where y ∼ Bernoulli(Xa −⌊Xa⌋) and Xa is a constant. We tune each Xa on the validation sets so that the rule-based policy has the same average count of each action as the RL-learned policy.
comments.explanation: Matching the average number of action types is useful, but the exact random policy remains unclear. The paper does not specify whether actions are sampled uniformly among remaining-budget valid actions, how invalid SUB actions at full stack size are handled, how DONE is sampled when the stack is empty or nonempty, or whether DO is forced after request budgets are exhausted. These choices can materially affect a random baseline in a hierarchical stack process, so the procedure should be specified more fully.

comments.title: Equating GOAL-once/CUR-every-step with the dense-description baseline depends on an unstated assistant assumption
comments.quote: Compared to a policy that calls GOAL at the beginning and calls CUR at every step (which is equivalent to the dense-d[s]-and-d[g] baseline), our policy achieves higher success rate on UNSEENENV while making two times fewer requests.
comments.explanation: The equivalence is plausible if GOAL and CUR responses exactly match the dense goal and current-state descriptions used by the dense-description baseline. However, that is an experimental assumption rather than a consequence of the formal HARI definitions. The assistant response model could be stochastic, repeated CUR requests could differ from the dense observation function, and the dense baseline may receive dense descriptions without request actions or interaction-state changes. The paper should state explicitly that, in the experiment, assistant responses are identical to the dense d[s] and d[g] inputs used by that baseline, apart from request accounting.

comments.title: Claim of statistical significance is ambiguous without evidence of statistical testing
comments.quote: The RL-learned interaction policy is significantly more effective than the rule-based baseline (+7.1% on UNSEENENV).
comments.explanation: The word “significantly” can be read as a statistical claim, but the passage does not report confidence intervals, standard errors, numbers of evaluation tasks, bootstrap tests, or hypothesis tests for the +7.1 percentage-point improvement. If the authors mean practical significance, “substantially” would avoid ambiguity. If they mean statistical significance, the supporting evidence should be reported.

comments.title: Possible inconsistency between reported SUB averages in text and Table 2 needs clarification
comments.quote: we find that the agent makes more SUB requests (averagely 4.5 requests per episode compared to 1.0 request made when the stack size is 2)
comments.explanation: The prose reports 1.0 and 4.5 SUB requests, while Table 2 reports average SUB counts of 1.7 for stack size 2 and 5.0 for stack size 3. A possible explanation is that the prose refers specifically to UNSEENOBJ while the table's action-count columns are pooled over validation conditions, but this is not made explicit. The paper should clarify the averaging population for both the table and the diagnostic sentence.

comments.title: Causal explanation for overusing SUB is stronger than the presented evidence supports
comments.quote: These results show that the critic model overestimates the V values in states where SUB actions are taken, leading to the agent learning to request subgoals more than needed.
comments.explanation: The reported results show that larger stack sizes lead to more SUB requests and do not consistently improve success, suggesting behavioral overuse. But they do not by themselves establish the mechanism as critic value overestimation in SUB states. Supporting that causal claim would require additional evidence, such as comparing predicted values to empirical returns, showing biased advantages for SUB, or ruling out alternative causes such as reward design, exploration, partial observability, representation limits, or distribution shift.

comments.title: “Strict extension” of prior subgoal/advice frameworks is stronger than what is established
comments.quote: HARI can be viewed as a strict extension of these frameworks.
comments.explanation: HARI plausibly broadens the communication interface by adding requests for current-state and goal-state information alongside subgoal advice. However, “strict extension” suggests that prior frameworks are formally recoverable as special cases of HARI. The paper does not show that the cited frameworks, which may have different advice semantics, teacher policies, or objectives, embed into the proposed POMDP construction. A safer claim would be that HARI generalizes aspects of these frameworks or subsumes subgoal-style advice at the interface level.

comments.title: The connection between faithful explanations and more specific questions is underspecified
comments.quote: Furthermore, techniques for generating faithful explanations (Kumar & Talukdar, 2020; Madsen et al., 2021) can be applied to enhance the specificity of the generated questions.
comments.explanation: The proposed future direction is plausible, but the connection is not immediate in the current framework. HARI selects among a small set of discrete communication actions such as CUR, GOAL, and SUB; it does not generate open-ended natural-language questions. Explanation-generation methods usually produce rationales or attributions for model decisions, not information-seeking queries. Applying them here would require an additional mechanism mapping explanations or uncertainty attributions into question content and likely an expanded utterance/action space.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important and timely problem—agents that can recognize uncertainty and solicit targeted human assistance—and the proposed HARI framework is conceptually appealing, especially in distinguishing *when* to ask from *what* to ask and in allowing requests for current-state, goal-state, and subgoal information. The introduction is generally clear and motivates the work well, with a useful connection to cooperative communication and POMDP limitations, and the navigation example helps make the framework concrete. That said, the framing currently overstates the novelty and faithfulness of the communication mechanism relative to what is demonstrated: the “human” appears simulated, the request types are hand-designed and limited, and it is not yet clear that the agent is learning grounded communicative intent rather than optimizing over a small set of oracle-like information channels. The claims about rich human-agent communication and Gricean faithfulness therefore need to be tied more carefully to the actual experimental setup. A major concern is whether the framework’s empirical gains come from principled hierarchical interaction learning or from privileged access to structured side information, especially subgoals, whose generation process and realism must be scrutinized. The paper would benefit from sharper formal definitions early on, clearer separation between the general framework and the specific navigation instantiation, stronger discussion of assumptions about the assistant’s knowledge and reliability, and comparisons to simpler baselines such as fixed query schedules, uncertainty-triggered querying, or agents with equivalent additional observations. Overall, the paper has a promising idea and readable motivation, but its contribution will depend heavily on whether the formalization and experiments convincingly show that the learned interaction policy provides benefits beyond access to carefully engineered help signals.

comments.title: Goal-conditioned tasks are not fully encoded in the stated POMDP cost/state formalism
comments.quote: We consider an environment defined by a partially-observed Markov decision process (POMDP) _E_ = ( _S, A, T, c, D, ρ_ ) with state space _S_ , action space _A_ , state-transition function _T_ : _S × A →_ ∆( _S_ ), cost function _c_ : _S × A →_ R, description space _D_ , and description function _ρ_ : _S →_ ∆( _D_ ).
comments.explanation: The authors are clearly trying to set up a goal-conditioned POMDP in which the physical operation state is in S, while the goal state g_1 and its description d[g]_1 are supplied by the task distribution and passed separately to the policy. That intent is reinforced later by the policy type \hat{π}: B × D → Δ(A), and in the interaction-environment construction the true goal stack is explicitly included in the state. However, in the formal POMDP given here, the cost is c:S×A→R and the belief state is later described as a distribution over S only. Since tasks may have different sampled goals g_1, the objective “reach g_1” cannot generally be represented by a single task-independent cost c(s,a) unless the goal is somehow included in the state, the cost is actually goal/task-dependent, or the objective has an additional success constraint/terminal penalty not shown here. Otherwise, for nonnegative action costs, minimizing cumulative cost need not incentivize reaching the particular sampled goal at all. A standard fix would be to augment the POMDP state with the task/goal, use c_g(s,a) or c(s,g,a), or state explicitly that c is task-dependent despite the simplified notation.

comments.title: Trajectory indexing appears inconsistent with an H-step cost sum
comments.quote: The goal of the agent is to reach _g_ 1 with minimum cumulative cost _C_ ( _τ_ ) =[] _[H] t_ =1 _[c][t]_[, where] _[ τ]_[= (] _[s]_[1] _[, d][s]_ 1 _[, a]_[1] _[, s]_[2] _[, d][s]_ 2 _[, a]_[2] _[, . . . , s][H][, d][s] H_[)][ is an execution of the task.]
comments.explanation: I understand the intended construction: at each time t the agent observes d[s]_t, chooses a_t, transitions to s_{t+1}, and incurs c_t. Under that convention, an H-step rollout with costs summed for t=1,…,H would normally include actions a_1,…,a_H and either states through s_{H+1} or an explicitly terminal convention. As written, τ stops at (s_H,d[s]_H) and omits a_H and the post-action state/description, even though C(τ) includes c_H and the preceding paragraph says a_t causes a transition to s_{t+1}. This is likely only an indexing/slight notation slip, but it makes the definition of P_π over executions and the horizon convention ambiguous. If instead the authors intend H observations and only H−1 actions, the cost sum should be adjusted accordingly.

comments.title: The claimed limitation of the standard POMDP framework is overstated
comments.quote: Thus, the standard POMDP framework does not provide any mechanism for improving the agent’s performance other than enhancing the operation policy.
comments.explanation: Within the specific operation environment just defined—fixed description function ρ, fixed initial goal description d[g]_1, and no assistant/query actions—the conclusion is reasonable: performance is determined by the learned operation policy. However, the statement is broader than what the formal setup establishes. Standard POMDPs can model information-gathering actions, active sensing, action-dependent observation models, external communication channels, or an assistant by augmenting the state, action, and observation spaces. In that sense, the limitation is not inherent to the POMDP framework itself but to the restricted operation POMDP the authors choose as their baseline. The point would be more precise if qualified as “this fixed operation POMDP” or “a standard autonomous formulation without query actions,” rather than the standard POMDP framework in general.

comments.title: The DO macro-action makes the interaction POMDP transition and cost history-dependent as written
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: DO is a high-level interaction action that delegates one step to the already learned operation policy, and this is a natural way to compose the interaction policy with \(\hat\pi\). However, after checking the formal interaction-environment definition, the induced transition is not a standard POMDP kernel under the stated state and action spaces. When the interaction action is merely DO, the actual low-level operation action is \(a^{do}_t=\arg\max_a \hat\pi(a\mid b^s_t,d^g_t)\). This depends on the operation-policy memory/belief \(b^s_t\) and the current top goal description \(d^g_t\), not just on \((s_t,d^s_t)\) and the abstract action DO. The proposed \(T_s:S\times D\times \bar A\to\Delta(S\times D)\) therefore lacks at least the stack/top goal description, and more importantly lacks the controller memory/history needed to compute \(a^{do}_t\). The same issue appears in the cost definition for DO, because \(\bar c\) uses \(c(s_t,a^{do}_t)\), but \(a^{do}_t\) is not determined by \((s_t,G_t,\mathrm{DO})\) unless the relevant operation belief is included in the state or action. A standard fix would be to augment the interaction state with the operation-policy hidden state/belief, or to define the interaction action as a parameterized low-level action such as DO\((a)\). As written, the claimed interaction environment is not quite a Markov/POMDP environment.

comments.title: Goal-stack transition for SUB is not determined by stack and action alone
comments.quote:
The SUB action pushes a new subtask ( _gt_ +1 _, d[g] t_ +1[)][ to the] stack.

The goal-stack transition function _TG_ is defined as _TG_ ( _Gt_ +1 _| Gt,_ ¯ _at_ ) = 1 _{Gt_ +1 = _Gt._ `update` (¯ _at_ ) _}_ where 1 _{.}_ is an indicator function.
comments.explanation: I read this as an attempt to isolate stack bookkeeping from the operation-state dynamics, which is a useful abstraction. But the earlier assistant model makes the SUB stack update stochastic and dependent on the current operation state: \(g_{t+1}\sim \omega_A(\cdot\mid s_t,g_t)\) and then \(d^g_{t+1}\sim \rho_A(\cdot\mid g_{t+1},\emptyset)\). Thus the next stack under SUB cannot be computed from \(G_t\) and \(\bar a_t\) alone; it also depends on \(s_t\) and on the assistant’s random draws. GOAL is closer to being stack-local because the current goal state and description are stored in \(G_t\), but SUB is not. The deterministic indicator form \(1\{G_{t+1}=G_t.\texttt{update}(\bar a_t)\}\) also hides the stochastic assistant distribution. This could be repaired by defining \(T_G(G_{t+1}\mid s_t,G_t,\bar a_t)\) and spelling out the assistant-induced probabilities. Without that, the factorization \(\bar T=T_s\cdot T_G\) is under-specified.

comments.title: Belief state is described as summarizing hidden states rather than observations
comments.quote: A belief state[¯] _bt_ summarizes a partial execution (¯ _s_ 1 _,_ ¯ _a_ 1 _, · · · ,_ ¯ _st_ ). We formally define the interaction policy as _ψθ_ : _B_[¯] _→_ ∆( _A_ ), where _B_[¯] is the set of all interaction belief states.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly rely on the standard POMDP idea that a belief summarizes the information available from the interaction history. What is confusing here is that the displayed partial execution contains \(\bar s_1,\ldots,\bar s_t\), i.e. interaction-environment states, even though those states include the true operation state and true goal stack and are explicitly not fully observable. Earlier in the paper, the observable partial execution was defined using descriptions and actions, not true states. In a POMDP, the belief is a distribution over hidden states conditioned on the observation/action history; it is not computed from a history of hidden states available to the agent. This is likely a notation slip, but it matters because the interaction policy’s input is being formally specified here. Replacing the sequence with observed descriptions/goal-description stacks and actions would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Goal-description stack length is inconsistent with stacks containing at most L elements
comments.quote: Description space _D_[¯] = _D × GL[d]_[where] _[ G] L[d]_[is the set of all goal-description stacks of size] _[ L]_[.] At any time, the agent cannot access the environment’s goal stack _G_ , which contains true goal states. Instead, it can only observe the descriptions in _G_ .
comments.explanation: The construction sensibly distinguishes the true goal stack from the observable stack of goal descriptions. However, the earlier state-space definition says \(GL\) contains stacks with at most \(L\) elements, and the initial stack has only one element. Here \(G_L^d\) is described as containing goal-description stacks of size \(L\). If the authors intend fixed-length padded stacks, that should be stated, including what padding value is observed and how it interacts with DONE/SUB. If they intend variable-length stacks up to depth \(L\), then “of size \(L\)” should be “of size at most \(L\).” This is a small but real ambiguity because the observation space and boundary behavior of an empty or partially filled stack are part of the formal POMDP definition.

comments.title: Navigation action space is state-dependent and appears to omit termination
comments.quote: At any time, the agent’s operation action space _A_ consists of traversing to any of the nodes that are adjacent to its current node.
comments.explanation: I first interpreted this as a standard graph-navigation shorthand: from a node, the physically valid moves are to neighboring nodes. However, in the formal POMDP setup earlier, _A_ is a fixed action space and the operation policy is defined as \(\hat{\pi}:B\times D\to\Delta(A)\), with DO choosing \(\arg\max_{a\in A}\hat{\pi}(a\mid b^s_t,d^g_t)\). If _A_ now consists only of adjacent nodes, then the available action set varies with the true current node. That raises two unresolved technical details: how the policy outputs over a variable action set, and how the agent knows or is given the currently navigable neighbors under partial observability. In embodied-navigation work this is often handled by an action mask or by including navigable viewpoints in the observation, but that is not stated here. The sentence also seems to exclude the special termination action \(a_{\mathrm{done}}\) introduced earlier; even if HARI uses the interaction-level DONE action, the pre-training phase and the no-assistant operation-policy baselines need some specified stopping mechanism or horizon rule. This is likely fixable, but as written it conflicts with the earlier fixed-action POMDP formulation and leaves an important implementation detail ambiguous.

comments.title: Frame of reference for dense goal descriptions is ambiguous
comments.quote: A dense description of a current location contains the room name at the location, and the features of _M_ objects restricted to be within _δ_ meters of the location. The features of each object consists of (i) its name, (ii) horizontal and vertical angles (relative to the current viewpoint), and (iii) distance (in meters) from the object to the current location. A dense description of a goal follows the same representation scheme.
comments.explanation: The current-location description is clear: object angles and distances are measured relative to the agent's current viewpoint. I initially assumed that a goal description analogously describes objects around the goal node. But the phrase “relative to the current viewpoint” does not transfer cleanly to a goal description, because the goal is not generally observed from the agent's current viewpoint. If the goal description uses angles/distances relative to the goal viewpoint, that should be stated; if it uses angles/distances from the agent to objects at the goal, that would provide very different information and could leak navigational guidance. Since these features are direct inputs to the operation and interaction policies, the coordinate frame is a reproducibility-relevant detail rather than a purely linguistic ambiguity.

comments.title: Sparse goal description may either be underspecified or leak extra geometric information
comments.quote: The sparse goal description (the task request) has only features of the target object and the room name where the object is located at.
comments.explanation: The intended task request seems to be something like “find a mug in a kitchen,” i.e., object category plus room category. However, the immediately preceding definition says that an object's “features” include not only its name but also horizontal angle, vertical angle, and distance. If “features of the target object” includes those geometric quantities, then the sparse task request contains more than a normal object-and-room request and may partially reveal the target viewpoint. If instead the sparse goal contains only the target object name and room name, the word “features” is misleading and the representation should be made explicit. This matters because the distinction between sparse and dense goal descriptions is central to the reported benefit of GOAL requests.

comments.title: Adjacent-subgoal descriptions use oracle low-level actions rather than the stated dense-description format
comments.quote: Especially, if a subgoal location is adjacent or coincides with the agent’s current location, instead of describing room and object features, the human specifies the ground-truth action to go to the subgoal (an action is specified by its horizontal and vertical angles, and travel distance).[4]
comments.explanation: I can see the practical motivation: if a destination is immediately visible, a human might naturally say “go there” rather than describe surrounding objects. Still, this is a qualitatively different form of assistance from the dense room/object descriptions otherwise described. It gives the ground-truth low-level action to the subgoal, which can be much stronger than a state description and may substantially affect the measured value of SUB requests. It is also unclear how this action-valued goal description is represented to the pre-trained operation policy, which was said to be trained with dense descriptions of current locations and goals. The special case where the subgoal coincides with the current location is also underspecified: the “ground-truth action” might be DONE/no-op rather than a traversal action. The paper’s general framework allows arbitrary descriptions, so this is not conceptually invalid, but the experimental method should spell out how these oracle action descriptions are encoded and consumed.

comments.title: “Human always returns dense descriptions” is not quite consistent with the adjacent-subgoal exception
comments.quote: The human always returns dense descriptions.
comments.explanation: This sentence is easy to understand as a summary of the CUR/GOAL/SUB assistance protocol: the agent perceives sparse descriptions and receives dense information from the human. But just above, the text states that for adjacent or coincident subgoals the human returns a ground-truth action instead of room and object features. That may be intended as a special kind of dense subgoal description, but as written the two statements conflict. The issue is minor if the action-command case is simply treated as an additional dense-description format, but the exception should be acknowledged because it affects what information the assistant is allowed to provide.

comments.title: Unclear whether UNSEENENV is unseen to the interaction policy or only to the pre-trained operation policy
comments.quote: The interaction policy is trained in environments that are previously seen as well as unseen during pre-training. Finally, in the _evaluation_ phase, the interaction policy is tested on three conditions: seen environment and target object type but starting from a new room (UNSEENSTR), seen environment but new target object type (UNSEENOBJ), and new environment (UNSEENENV).
comments.explanation: I initially read UNSEENENV as a standard held-out-environment test condition. However, the preceding sentence says that the interaction policy is trained in environments that were unseen during operation-policy pre-training. That is reasonable for adapting the interaction policy, but it makes the reference point for “new environment” important. Is UNSEENENV new relative to pre-training only, or also held out from interaction-policy training? The paper’s broader claim concerns generalization to unseen environments, so a careful reader needs to know which learned component has or has not seen those environments. This may be clarified in the appendix splits, but in the main experimental description the distinction is ambiguous.

comments.title: Rule-based baseline is underspecified despite being a key comparison
comments.quote: We compare our RL-learned interaction policy with a rule-based baseline that first takes the GOAL action and then randomly selects actions. In each episode, we enforce that the rule-based policy can take at most _⌊Xa⌋_ + _y_ actions of type _a_ , where _y ∼_ Bernoulli( _Xa −⌊Xa⌋_ ) and _Xa_ is a constant. We tune each _Xa_ on the validation sets so that the rule-based policy has the same average count of each action as the RL-learned policy.
comments.explanation: The authors are careful to match the average number of action types, which is a useful attempt to separate the benefit of learned decision-making from simply asking more often. What remains unclear is the exact random policy being used once those budgets are imposed: whether actions are sampled uniformly from all remaining-budget valid actions, how invalid SUB actions at full stack size are handled, how DONE is sampled when the stack is empty or nonempty, and whether DO is forced when all request budgets are exhausted. These choices can materially affect a random baseline in a hierarchical stack process. Since Table 1 uses this baseline to support the claim that the learned interaction policy asks for the right information at the right time, the baseline procedure should be specified more fully.

comments.title: Claim that UNSEENENV goal descriptions are “completely new” may overstate what the split guarantees
comments.quote: In contrast, the initial goal descriptions in UNSEENOBJ and UNSEENENV are completely new to the agent, thus gathering more information about them is necessary.
comments.explanation: For UNSEENOBJ, this explanation is straightforward: the target object type is new, so the initial sparse goal description plausibly contains a novel object category. For UNSEENENV, however, “new environment” does not by itself imply that the sparse initial goal description is completely new, especially since the sparse request appears to consist mainly of object type and room name. The specific house, target node, or object instance may be new, but the object and room categories may have been seen before. If the authors mean that the exact goal instance or environment-specific goal representation is new, that should be stated more narrowly. As written, the sentence may overstate the reason dense goal information helps in UNSEENENV.

comments.title: Causal explanation for overusing SUB is stronger than the presented evidence supports
comments.quote: These results show that the critic model overestimates the _V_ values in states where SUB actions are taken, leading to the agent learning to request subgoals more than needed.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended interpretation: Table 2 shows that increasing stack size causes many more SUB requests, while success on UNSEENOBJ does not improve and success on UNSEENSTR declines, so the learned policy appears to overvalue subgoal requests in some regimes. That is a useful diagnostic. However, the table itself only demonstrates behavioral overuse of SUB under the learned policy; it does not by itself identify the mechanism as critic overestimation of the value function in SUB states. In actor-critic RL this is a plausible failure mode, but establishing it would normally require additional evidence, such as comparing predicted values to empirical returns for states before/after SUB, showing positive advantage bias for SUB, or ruling out alternative causes such as reward/cost misspecification, exploration bias, partial observability, representation limits, or distribution shift from deeper stacks. As written, the sentence states a specific internal cause as something the results 'show', which seems stronger than what the reported results justify.

comments.title: Possible inconsistency between reported SUB averages in text and Table 2 needs clarification
comments.quote: we find that the agent makes more SUB requests (averagely 4.5 requests per episode compared to 1.0 request made when the stack size is 2)
comments.explanation: I initially compared this statement to Table 2, where the average number of SUB actions is reported as 1.7 for stack size 2 and 5.0 for stack size 3, not 1.0 and 4.5. A possible resolution is that the prose is specifically discussing UNSEENOBJ, whereas the action-count columns in Table 2 may be averaged over all validation conditions. If so, the numbers are not necessarily contradictory. However, the table header says the results are 'on validation' and does not clearly state whether the action counts are pooled over all task types or correspond to one of the preceding success-rate columns, while the prose switches to an UNSEENOBJ-specific diagnostic without saying explicitly that these 1.0/4.5 figures are condition-specific and not the table values. A careful reader could reasonably think the prose is summarizing Table 2 and be confused by the mismatch. Clarifying the averaging population for both the table and the sentence would resolve this.

comments.title: Claim of statistical significance is ambiguous without evidence of statistical testing
comments.quote:
The RL-learned interaction policy is significantly more effective than

the rule-based baseline (+7.1% on UNSEENENV).
comments.explanation: The comparison is clear in spirit: the learned interaction policy improves over a rule-based baseline by 7.1 percentage points on UNSEENENV, which may be practically meaningful. My concern is with the word 'significantly'. In empirical ML papers this can be read either colloquially ('substantially') or statistically ('statistically significant'). The passage does not mention confidence intervals, standard errors, number of evaluation tasks, bootstrap tests, or hypothesis tests around this +7.1% difference. If the authors intend only practical significance, a word like 'substantially' would avoid overclaiming. If they intend statistical significance, the evidence should be reported.

comments.title: Equating GOAL-once/CUR-every-step with the dense-description baseline depends on an unstated assistant assumption
comments.quote: Compared to a policy that calls GOAL at the beginning and calls CUR at every step (which is equivalent to the dense- _d[s]_ -and- _d[g]_ baseline), our policy achieves higher success rate on UNSEENENV while making two times fewer requests.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended equivalence: if the assistant returns dense descriptions for GOAL and CUR, then asking GOAL once and CUR at every step gives the operation policy the same current-state and goal information as the dense-description baseline. This is plausible in the HAN setup, where dense descriptions are defined and human requests can provide additional descriptions. Still, the equivalence is not automatic from the formal HARI definitions: \(\rho_A\) could be stochastic, repeated CUR requests could produce descriptions with different noise or content than the dense observation function, and the dense baseline may receive dense current descriptions without incurring request actions or interaction-policy state changes. The passage would be safer if it explicitly stated the experimental assumption under which the assistant's responses exactly match the dense \(d^s\) and \(d^g\) inputs used by that baseline, apart from request costs/action counts.

comments.title: “Strict extension” of prior subgoal/advice frameworks is stronger than what is established
comments.quote: HARI can be viewed as a strict extension of these frameworks.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: prior action-advising work typically transfers low-level actions, and some newer work allows teachers to provide high-level subgoals, whereas HARI adds additional request types for current-state and goal-state information. In that informal sense, HARI broadens the communication interface. However, the phrase “strict extension” has a fairly strong technical meaning: it suggests that the earlier frameworks are recoverable as special cases of HARI under some parameterization or restriction. From the definitions given earlier, HARI has a fixed interaction action space \(\{\mathrm{CUR},\mathrm{GOAL},\mathrm{SUB},\mathrm{DO},\mathrm{DONE}\}\), an assistant specified by \(\rho_A\) and \(\omega_A\), and a black-box operation policy \(\hat\pi\). It is plausible that subgoal-advice frameworks can be approximated by disabling CUR/GOAL and using SUB, but the paper does not show that the referenced frameworks—some of which learn teaching policies or use different advice semantics/objectives—embed formally into this POMDP construction. The concern is not with the novelty claim itself, but with the word “strict,” which overstates what has been demonstrated. A safer claim would be that HARI generalizes aspects of these frameworks or subsumes subgoal-style advice at the interface level.

comments.title: The connection between faithful explanations and more specific questions is underspecified
comments.quote: Furthermore, techniques for generating faithful explanations (Kumar & Talukdar, 2020; Madsen et al., 2021) can be applied to enhance the specificity of the generated questions.
comments.explanation: I see the authors’ intended future direction: if the agent can explain what it is uncertain about or why its policy is failing, then it might ask more targeted questions. This is consistent with the paper’s motivation around faithful communication. However, in the formal HARI framework and the navigation experiments described earlier, the agent does not actually generate natural-language questions; it selects among a small discrete set of communication intentions, such as CUR, GOAL, and SUB. Explanation-generation methods also typically produce rationales for model decisions, not necessarily information-seeking queries. Thus the stated application is plausible but not immediate: it would require an additional mechanism mapping faithful explanations or uncertainty attributions into question content, and likely an expanded action/utterance space. Since this appears in the conclusion as a speculative future direction, it is not a methodological flaw, but a careful reader could reasonably find the claim under-justified as written.
